quiz_questions.question_text: What is the moment of inertia of a point mass of 3 kg located 2 m from the axis of rotation?
What is the moment of inertia of a solid disk of mass 4 kg and radius 0.5 m about its central axis?
A thin hoop of mass 2 kg and radius 0.3 m rotates about its central axis. What is its moment of inertia?
quiz_questions.explanation: For a hoop: I = MR² = 2 × (0.3)² = 2 × 0.09 = 0.18 kg·m².

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the parallel axis theorem, if a disk has I_cm = 0.5 kg·m², mass 4 kg, and is rotated about an axis 1 m from its center, what is I?
quiz_questions.explanation: Parallel axis theorem: I = I_cm + Md² = 0.5 + 4(1²) = 0.5 + 4 = 4.5 kg·m².

quiz_questions.question_text: A solid sphere has mass 5 kg and radius 0.2 m. What is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
A uniform thin rod of mass 3 kg and length 2 m is rotated about its center. What is its moment of inertia?
The same 3 kg, 2 m rod is rotated about one end. What is its moment of inertia?
Two point masses, 2 kg each, are connected by a massless rod 1 m long and rotate about the center. What is the total I?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each mass is 0.5 m from the center. I_total = m₁r₁² + m₂r₂² = 2(0.5)² + 2(0.5)² = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 kg·m².

quiz_questions.question_text: Moment of inertia depends on which two factors?

options.text: Mass distribution and axis of rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Moment of inertia depends on how mass is distributed and where the rotation axis is located. Moving mass farther from the axis increases I.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hollow sphere of mass 6 kg and radius 0.1 m has I about its center equal to:
quiz_questions.explanation: For a hollow (thin-walled) sphere: I = (2/3)MR² = (2/3)(6)(0.01) = 0.04 kg·m².

quiz_questions.question_text: What is moment of inertia?

options.text: The kinetic energy of a spinning object
The resistance of a body to changes in its rotational motion; the rotational analog of mass
The angular velocity of a rotating object
The torque needed to maintain constant angular velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Moment of inertia (I) is the rotational analog of mass. It quantifies how difficult it is to change an object's rotational motion, depending on mass distribution relative to the axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the parallel axis theorem?

options.text: I = ½MR² for all disks due to the well-established relationship between axis trends and broader outcomes
I = I_cm + Md²; the moment of inertia about any axis equals the moment about a parallel axis through the center of mass plus Md²
The moment of inertia is the same about all parallel axes regardless of prevailing conditions in practice
I = I_cm − Md² for any parallel axis in most scenarios involving axis factors

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel axis theorem states I = I_cm + Md², where d is the distance between the center-of-mass axis and the new parallel axis. It always increases I relative to the center of mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the perpendicular axis theorem?

options.text: The moment of inertia is always largest about the perpendicular axis
For a planar body: I_z = I_x + I_y, where z is perpendicular to the plane containing x and y
I = MR² for any axis perpendicular to a disk
I about any axis equals the sum of I about two other axes

quiz_questions.explanation: The perpendicular axis theorem applies to planar (flat) objects: I_z = I_x + I_y, where z is perpendicular to the plane and x, y are in the plane.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'radius of gyration' mean?

options.text: The distance k from the axis at which a point mass M would have the same moment of inertia (I = Mk²)
The actual radius of a rotating object
Half the diameter of the rotating body
The maximum radius of rotation before an object breaks apart

quiz_questions.explanation: The radius of gyration k is defined by I = Mk². It represents the effective distance from the axis at which the total mass could be concentrated to give the same I.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for moment of inertia to be an 'additive' quantity?

options.text: All parts of an object contribute equally to I
You can multiply the moments of inertia of parts
Moment of inertia always increases when objects are combined
The total I of a composite body equals the sum of I values of its individual parts about the same axis

quiz_questions.explanation: Moment of inertia is additive: for a composite object, I_total = I_1 + I_2 + ... about the same axis. This allows calculation of complex shapes by summing simpler components.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'principal axis' of rotation?

options.text: The axis with the maximum moment of inertia
The axis perpendicular to the angular velocity
Any axis through the center of mass
An axis about which the object can rotate without wobbling; the products of inertia vanish

quiz_questions.explanation: A principal axis is one about which the angular momentum and angular velocity are parallel. Products of inertia are zero, and the object rotates without wobbling.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for the moment of inertia of a continuous body?

options.text: I = Σ m_i r_i, as conventional continuous theory
I = ∫ F dr when viewed through a simplified
I = ∫ r² dm; the integral of r² over all mass elements
I = M × R under most circumstances involving

quiz_questions.explanation: For a continuous mass distribution, I = ∫ r² dm, where r is the perpendicular distance from each mass element dm to the axis of rotation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mass distribution' refer to in the context of rotational inertia?

options.text: The shape of the object only
How the mass of an object is spread relative to the axis of rotation
The total mass of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass distribution describes how mass is arranged relative to the rotation axis. Objects with more mass farther from the axis have larger moments of inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a solid cylinder and a hollow cylinder of the same mass and radius in terms of I?

options.text: The solid cylinder has a larger I
It depends on the height of the cylinders
The hollow cylinder has a larger I because its mass is concentrated farther from the axis

quiz_questions.explanation: Solid cylinder: I = ½MR². Hollow (thin-walled) cylinder: I = MR². The hollow cylinder has twice the I because all mass is at maximum distance from the axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'rotational inertia' emphasize?

options.text: The angular momentum of the object
An object's tendency to resist changes in its rotational state of motion
The force required to stop rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Rotational inertia emphasizes an object's resistance to angular acceleration. A larger I means more torque is needed to achieve the same angular acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A potter's wheel is a solid disk of mass 40 kg and radius 0.3 m. What torque is needed to accelerate it from rest to 5 rev/s in 4 s?

A figure skater (modeled as a cylinder, mass 50 kg, radius 0.2 m) spins with arms tucked. She extends her arms, increasing her effective radius to 0.6 m. By what factor does her moment of inertia change?
quiz_questions.explanation: I = ½MR². The ratio is R₂²/R₁² = (0.6)²/(0.2)² = 0.36/0.04 = 9. Her moment of inertia increases by a factor of 9.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg uniform disk (R = 0.15 m) is mounted on an axle 0.25 m from its center using the parallel axis theorem. What is I about the axle?
Two identical solid spheres (each 1 kg, radius 0.05 m) are connected by a 0.4 m massless rod and rotated about the center of the rod. What is I_total?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each sphere: I_cm = (2/5)(1)(0.0025) = 0.001 kg·m². Distance from center of rod to sphere center: d = 0.2 m. By parallel axis theorem: I_each = 0.001 + 1(0.04) = 0.041. I_total = 2 × 0.041 = 0.082 kg·m².

quiz_questions.question_text: A flywheel has I = 8 kg·m² and rotates at 300 RPM. What is its rotational kinetic energy?
A composite object consists of a solid disk (mass 3 kg, radius 0.2 m) with a point mass of 1 kg attached at the rim. What is the total I about the disk's center?
A solid ball and a hollow ball of the same mass and radius roll down a ramp. Which reaches the bottom first and why?

options.text: The solid ball, because it has a smaller moment of inertia relative to its mass (I = 2/5 MR² vs 2/3 MR²)
It depends on the angle of the ramp
The hollow ball, because it has less rotational inertia
They arrive at the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: The solid ball has I = (2/5)MR² while the hollow ball has I = (2/3)MR². A smaller I/MR² ratio means less energy goes into rotation and more into translation, so the solid ball accelerates faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer needs to design a flywheel with I = 2 kg·m². She can use either a solid disk or a thin hoop. If the mass is 10 kg, what radius does each require?
quiz_questions.explanation: Disk: I = ½MR² → R = √(2I/M) = √(4/10) = √0.4 = 0.632 m. Hoop: I = MR² → R = √(I/M) = √(2/10) = √0.2 = 0.447 m. The hoop needs a smaller radius since all mass is at the rim.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.5 kg record (thin disk, R = 0.15 m) on a turntable is acted on by a frictional torque of 0.003 N·m. Starting at 33⅓ RPM, how long does it take to stop?
A uniform door (mass 12 kg, width 0.9 m) is modeled as a thin rectangular slab rotating about its hinges. What is its moment of inertia? (I = ⅓ML² for a rod about one end)
quiz_questions.explanation: Treating the door as a slab rotating about one edge: I = (1/3)Mw² = (1/3)(12)(0.81) = (1/3)(9.72) = 3.24 kg·m².
